definition: Low-level JavaScript API for rendering 3D graphics using the GPU — the foundation that Three.js is built upon
The 3D world container that holds all objects, lights, and cameras in Three.js
Defines the viewpoint from which the scene is rendered — perspective or orthographic
Takes the scene and camera and draws them onto an HTML canvas element using WebGL
A 3D object that combines geometry (shape) and material (appearance)
The shape and structure of a 3D object, defined by vertices and faces
Defines how a surface looks — its color, texture, shininess, and other visual properties
The HTML element where Three.js renders 3D graphics
Built-in Three.js geometry for creating rectangular boxes and cubes
Simplest Three.js material — flat color with no lighting effects, good for debugging
Camera that mimics human eye perspective where farther objects appear smaller
The default Three.js renderer that uses WebGL to draw scenes to a canvas
Browser API for scheduling smooth animations synced with the screen refresh rate
Tree structure of parent-child relationships between 3D objects where children inherit parent transforms
Width divided by height of the viewport — prevents distortion in the camera view
A single point in 3D space defined by x, y, and z coordinates
A triangle formed by three vertices — the basic unit of all 3D surfaces
Number of subdivisions in a geometry — higher values produce smoother curves but more polygons
Modern efficient geometry format in Three.js that stores vertex data in typed arrays for direct GPU transfer
Total number of triangular faces in a 3D model — lower counts mean better performance
Unit for measuring angles where pi radians equals 180 degrees
A position, rotation, or scale operation applied to a 3D object
Light scattered equally in all directions from a surface, creating a matte appearance
Light reflected in a mirror-like direction, creating shiny highlights on surfaces
Physically-Based Rendering — a realistic light simulation approach used by MeshStandardMaterial
2D coordinates (0-1 range) that define how a texture image maps onto 3D geometry
The base color of a surface without any lighting applied, also called the diffuse map
An RGB image encoding surface normals that fakes bumps and detail without adding geometry
PBR property controlling how metallic a surface appears — 0 is non-metal (plastic), 1 is full metal
PBR property controlling surface smoothness — 0 is mirror-like, 1 is completely matte
Light that illuminates all objects equally from all directions with no shadows
Light with parallel rays like sunlight — creates consistent shadows from an infinite distance
Light that radiates equally in all directions from a single point, like a light bulb
Focused cone of light like a flashlight, with configurable angle and soft edges
Brightness multiplier for lights — 1 is normal, 2 is double brightness
A depth texture rendered from a light's perspective, used to determine which areas are in shadow
The soft edge on a spotlight — 0 creates a hard edge, 1 creates a very soft falloff
How quickly a point or spot light fades with distance — a value of 2 is physically accurate
Field of View — the vertical viewing angle of a camera in degrees, controlling how wide or narrow the view is
The pyramid-shaped viewing volume between the camera's near and far clipping planes
Near and far boundaries of the camera — objects outside these distances are not rendered
Mouse and touch controls that let users rotate, pan, and zoom a camera around a target point
Inertia effect on camera controls — makes the camera continue moving smoothly after input stops
The point in 3D space that OrbitControls orbits around and the camera looks at
Professional lighting setup using a key light, fill light, and back light to create depth and definition
The process of wrapping 2D images onto 3D surfaces to add visual detail
Time elapsed since the last frame in seconds — used for frame-independent animation
Total time since the animation clock started, used for time-based repeating patterns
Three.js utility class for tracking time with getElapsedTime() and getDelta() methods
Non-linear interpolation that creates natural-feeling motion with acceleration and deceleration
Back-and-forth motion like a pendulum, typically created with Math.sin() or Math.cos()
Blending between two values using the formula a + (b - a) * t, where t ranges from 0 to 1
Making animation speed consistent regardless of the device's frame rate by using delta time
GL Transmission Format — the standard 3D file format for the web, often called the JPEG of 3D
Binary GLTF — a single-file variant that contains geometry, materials, and textures in one file
Three.js addon class for loading GLTF and GLB 3D model files into a scene
Decoder for DRACO-compressed 3D meshes that can reduce file sizes by 90% or more
Recorded animation data from a 3D model file, such as walk cycles or idle animations
The process of getting 3D models from creation tools like Blender to the web via Three.js
Shadow map type using percentage-closer filtering to create smooth, soft shadow edges
Small offset applied to shadow maps to prevent shadow acne (striping artifacts)
An HDR image wrapping the entire scene to provide natural, omnidirectional lighting and reflections
High Dynamic Range — image format capturing brightness values above 1.0 for realistic lighting
Process of compressing HDR brightness values into the displayable screen range
Overall brightness multiplier applied during tone mapping via toneMappingExposure
Soft shadows that appear where objects meet surfaces, adding a grounded look
A single GPU command to render a mesh — more draw calls mean more CPU overhead
Three.js class that renders many copies of the same geometry and material in a single draw call
Level of Detail — showing simpler models when objects are far from the camera to improve performance
Combining multiple static meshes into one to reduce draw calls, at the cost of individual control
Manually freeing GPU memory for unused geometries, materials, and textures to prevent memory leaks
Automatic optimization that skips rendering objects outside the camera's view
Debug overlay panel showing FPS, frame time, and memory usage in real time
Instructions sent to an LLM that define its role, rules, and output format for every request
Providing example input-output pairs to an LLM so it learns the desired response pattern
Asking an LLM to perform a task without providing any examples
LLM parameter controlling output randomness — 0 is deterministic, 1.0 is creative
When an AI invents non-existent things, like referencing geometry types that do not exist in Three.js
A defined structure for data output, such as a JSON format with specific required fields
Forcing an LLM response into a specific data format like JSON for reliable programmatic parsing
Prompting technique asking the AI to reason step-by-step before producing the final answer
A class that converts JSON scene descriptions into actual Three.js objects in the browser
A dictionary mapping string names like 'sphere' to Three.js geometry constructor functions
Handling missing or invalid data gracefully with sensible defaults instead of crashing
Translating natural language concepts like 'glowing' or 'metallic' into specific 3D material parameters
Restricting a value to a valid range using Math.max and Math.min
An end-to-end demo that proves all layers of an application work together
Selecting pre-built scene responses based on keyword detection in user input
Smooth visual change between scenes, such as fading out old objects and fading in new ones
HTML elements positioned on top of the 3D canvas for input fields, buttons, and status messages
Visual feedback shown during async operations like API calls or model loading
Secret credential that authenticates API requests to services like Claude or OpenAI
Unit of text processed by LLMs — roughly 4 characters equals 1 token
Maximum number of API requests allowed per minute or hour to prevent abuse
Receiving API responses incrementally token-by-token instead of waiting for the complete response
Array of user and assistant message pairs sent with each API call for multi-turn context
Stored preferences and context about the user that enhance AI-generated responses
Maximum context size an LLM can process in a single request — e.g., 200K tokens for Claude
Browser persistent storage that survives page reloads, used for saving user preferences
Temporary in-memory data that is cleared when the page reloads
Prompting pattern where the AI reviews and rates its own output to identify issues for refinement
Testing multiple temperature values (0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0) to find the best setting for a prompt
Breaking a complex prompting task into sequential steps like generate, critique, then refine
Configuration values that change per deployment environment, like API keys and feature flags
Backend code that runs on-demand without managing a server, used to hide API keys from the client
Build optimization that removes unused code from the final bundle to reduce file size
Breaking the application bundle into smaller chunks that can be loaded independently
Content Delivery Network — distributes your app globally for fast loading from any location
Monitoring production errors using tools like Sentry to catch and diagnose issues in real time
Camera with no perspective distortion where parallel lines stay parallel — used for 2D-style games or technical views
Three.js class for asynchronously loading image files and converting them to GPU-ready textures
PBR material using roughness and metalness for realistic rendering — the industry standard for quality
Material with specular highlights for shiny surfaces like plastic or polished objects
Material with diffuse reflection for matte surfaces like chalk or unpolished wood
A Three.js container object used to organize multiple meshes so they can be transformed together
The continuous cycle of updating scene state and rendering frames, typically running at 60 FPS
Using API features to guarantee the AI response matches a specific JSON structure
Determining what action the user wants based on their natural language input
Class that maintains rolling conversation history and prepares API payloads with context
Cinematic tone mapping algorithm that produces contrasty, film-like visuals from HDR lighting
